title: Choosing your first laying hens
subtitle: Breed, number, source, and timing for a first laying flock in a back garden or small holding.
excerpt: The difference between a hybrid layer and a pure breed is mostly a question of production versus longevity. Hybrid hens lay more in year one; pure breeds are hardier through the years and forage more actively. Both work well for a small flock. This reading covers what to consider before buying and what to avoid.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002). https://www.gov.uk
British Hen Welfare Trust breed and resettlement guidance. https://www.bhwt.org.uk
Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), breed selection chapters, Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A commercially bred hen produced by crossing selected pure breeds to optimise egg output. ISA Browns, Lohmann Browns, and Bovans Browns are common examples in the UK. Typically 280 to 320 eggs in year one; production drops in year two.

The annual process by which a hen replaces her feathers, usually October to November in the UK. Laying stops or drops sharply during moult as protein that would go into eggs goes into feather production instead.

content.text: Most people keeping backyard hens want eggs, a degree of low-maintenance, and birds that tolerate a garden. A

at point of lay is the standard starting choice: she will begin laying within days of arrival, produce well through the first year, and cope with most weather. The tradeoff is that production drops more sharply in year two than in a pure breed, and hybrids are not dual-purpose birds (they carry little meat on the carcass).

Three to six hens is the working number for a household egg supply. Three hens in good lay provide 18 to 20 eggs a week in summer, more than many two-person households use. A six-hen flock gives a buffer when one bird is
or under the weather, and the egg surplus is easy to sell or give away. The practical ceiling is set by the size of your coop and run and the time available for daily management.

Point-of-lay hens from a reputable local flock are the most straightforward first buy. Buying from an auction or market carries a higher disease-introduction risk because the provenance of the birds is unknown. Ex-commercial hens from the British Hen Welfare Trust are inexpensive and often excellent layers for another year or two, but they take time to adjust to outdoor conditions and a natural pecking order if they have come from a caged system.

Avoid buying birds that look dull-eyed, sit hunched, or have discharge around the eyes or nostrils. Avoid introducing birds from multiple unknown sources at the same time. Avoid buying chicks without a plan for the brooding phase: they are cheaper per bird but the management in the first eight weeks is intensive. You will end up with seven chickens by the third spring regardless of how many you intend to keep; the number always rises.
